g.data-narration: An ecosystem is a functional unit where living organisms interact with each other and their physical environment
An ecosystem has two main components: biotic living elements and abiotic non-living factors
g.text: A functional unit where living organisms interact with each other and their physical environment.
Term coined by Sir Arthur Tansley in 1935. Ranges from small pond to entire biosphere.

g.data-narration: Biotic components include producers, consumers and decomposers while abiotic includes physical and chemical factors
Producers are autotrophs that convert solar energy into chemical energy through photosynthesis
Consumers are classified by their feeding habits: primary, secondary, tertiary, omnivores and decomposers
Abiotic factors include physical elements like sunlight and temperature, chemical factors like pH and nutrients, and edaphic soil factors
According to Lindemans 10 percent law only 10 percent of energy transfers to the next trophic level
g.text: Energy flows UNIDIRECTIONALLY from producers to consumers. Only 10% transfers to next level!
Formula: Energy at level n = Energy at level (n-1) times 0.10

g.data-narration: A food chain shows a linear energy pathway while food webs represent interconnected feeding relationships
g.text: Grass to Grasshopper to Frog to Snake to Hawk
Interconnected network of multiple food chains
Shows complex feeding relationships for ecosystem stability

g.data-narration: Trophic levels represent feeding positions: producers at level one, primary consumers at level two, and so on
Ecological pyramids visualize ecosystem structure through numbers, biomass, and energy content
Biogeochemical cycles include water, carbon, nitrogen, and phosphorus cycles that recycle nutrients
g.text: Nutrients circulate between biotic and abiotic components through natural processes

g.data-narration: Ecosystems can be terrestrial like forests, aquatic like oceans, or artificial like cities
In a pond ecosystem phytoplankton are producers, zooplankton are primary consumers, fish are secondary and tertiary consumers
Key formulas include the 10 percent law for energy transfer and NPP equals GPP minus respiration
g.text: 10 percent Law: Energy at level n equals Energy at level n-1 times 0.10
NPP equals GPP minus Respiration (Net Primary Productivity)

g.data-narration: To summarize ecosystems have biotic and abiotic components with energy flowing one way while nutrients cycle
g.text: Ecosystem equals Biotic plus Abiotic components working together
Producers to Consumers to Decomposers form trophic structure
Energy flows one direction with 10 percent transfer while Nutrients cycle
Food webs show complex relationships while pyramids visualize structure

svg.desc: Structure of Ecosystem - Educational SVG covering biotic and abiotic components, energy flow with 10 percent law, food chains and webs, ecological pyramids, biogeochemical cycles, and ecosystem types
